---
title: " Bayesian inference in non-Gaussian time series with global-local
shrinkage process priors"
author: "Mattias Villani"
institute: "Department of Statistics, Stockholm University"

format:
  revealjs:
    # ── Theme ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    theme: [default, ../custom.scss]
    css: ../custom.css
    include-after-body: 
      - ../cursor.js
      - ../logo.js
      - ../hover-refs.html

    # ── Math: SVG output is resolution-independent ─────────────────────────────
    html-math-method:
      method: mathjax
      url: "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/mathjax@3/es5/tex-svg.js"

    # ── Resolution: more pixels → sharper rendering ────────────────────────────
    width:  1600
    height: 900
    margin: 0.05

    # ── Navigation ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    slide-number:     "c/t"
    progress:         true
    history:          false
    hash:             true
    controls:         false       # cleaner without arrow buttons
    transition:       none        # instant transitions look more professional
    transition-speed: fast

    # ── Code ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    highlight-style:  github
    code-line-numbers: false
    
    #footer: "NordStat2026"

    # ── PDF export (via decktape or browser print) ──────────────────────────────
    # Run:  quarto render talk.qmd --to revealjs
    # PDF:  open in browser → Print → Save as PDF (set margins to None)
    
resources:
  - figs/

# ── Execution ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
execute:
  echo:    true
  warning: false
  eval:    false

# ── Julia engine ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
jupyter: julia-1.12

# ── Bibliography ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
bibliography: references.bib
csl: vancouver.csl
---

```{=html}
<div style="display:none">
$$
\newcommand{\v}[1]{\boldsymbol{#1}}
\newcommand{\m}[1]{\mathbf{#1}}
$$
</div>
```


## Outline

1.  Dynamic shrinkage process priors
2.  Time-varying multi-seasonal ARMA
3.  Non-Gaussian models
4.  Posterior inference

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Time-varying parameter models

-   Regression with [time-varying parameters]{.alertred}

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{t} &= \v x_{t}^{\top}\v\beta_{t}+\varepsilon_{t},\qquad
\varepsilon_{t}\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}N(0,\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}) \\
\v\beta_{t} &= \v\beta_{t-1}+\boldsymbol{\nu}_{t},\qquad 
\v\nu_{t}\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}N(\v 0,\v\Sigma_{\nu})
\end{aligned}
$$
<div class="mediumskip"></div>

-   [Linear Gaussian state-space model]{.alertred} with state: $\v \theta_t=\v\beta_t$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Observation model:}\qquad\qquad\qquad \v y_{t} &=
\v C_t\v\theta_{t}+\v\varepsilon_{t},\qquad
&\v\varepsilon_{t}\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}N(\v 0,\v\Sigma_{\varepsilon,t}) \\
\text{Transition model:}\qquad\qquad\qquad\hspace{0.6cm} \v\theta_{t} 
&=\v A_t \v\theta_{t-1}+\boldsymbol{\nu}_{t},\qquad 
&\v\nu_{t}\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}N(\v 0,\v\Sigma_{\nu,t})
\end{aligned}
$$

## Taxonomy of state-space models

-   [Linear Gaussian state-space model]{.alertred} dgdgfdgdfgf dgfdgfdg fdg fdg fdg fd
$$
\begin{aligned}
\v y_{t} &=
\v C_t\v\theta_{t}+\v\varepsilon_{t}\qquad
&\v\varepsilon_{t}\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}N(\v 0,\v\Sigma_{\varepsilon,t}) \\
\v\theta_{t} 
&=\v A_t \v\theta_{t-1}+\boldsymbol{\nu}_{t}\qquad 
&\v\nu_{t}\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}N(\v 0,\v\Sigma_{\nu,t})
\end{aligned}
$$
<div class="mediumskip"></div>
-   [Non-linear (additive) Gaussian models]{.alertred} 
$$
\begin{aligned}
\v y_{t} &=
\v c_t(\v\theta_{t}) + \v\varepsilon_{t}\qquad
&\v\varepsilon_{t}\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}N(\v 0,\v\Sigma_{\varepsilon,t}) \\
\v\theta_{t} 
&=\v a_t(\v\theta_{t-1})+\boldsymbol{\nu}_{t}\qquad
&\v\nu_{t}\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}N(\v 0,\v\Sigma_{\nu,t})
\end{aligned}
$$
<div class="mediumskip"></div>
-   [General distributions - non-Gaussian]{.alertred} 
$$
\begin{aligned}
\v y_{t} &\sim p_t(\v y_{t} \vert \v\theta_t) \\
\v\theta_{t} &\sim q_t(\v\theta_{t} \vert \v\theta_{t-1})
\end{aligned}
$$
------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Bayesian inference in state-space models

<table style="width: 80%; margin: 0; border-collapse: collapse; text-align: left;  border: none;">
  <tr style="height: 140px;">
    <td style="width: 50%; vertical-align: bottom; padding-bottom: 3em; text-align: left;">
      <span class="alertred">Filtering posterior (instantaneous)</span> 
      
      $p(\boldsymbol{z}_{t}\vert y_{1:t})$
    </td>
    <td style="width: 50%; vertical-align: middle; text-align: center;">
      <img src="figs/filteringpost.svg" width="300">
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr style="height: 140px;">
    <td style="vertical-align: middle; padding-bottom: 3em;">
      <span class="alertred">Smoothing posterior (retrospective)</span> 
      
      $p(\boldsymbol{z}_{t}\vert y_{1:T})$
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: middle; text-align: center;">
      <img src="figs/smoothingpost.svg" width="300">
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr style="height: 140px;">
    <td style="vertical-align: middle; padding-bottom: 3em;">
      <span class="alertred">Joint smoothing posterior</span>
      
      $p(\boldsymbol{z}_{1:T}\vert y_{1:T})$
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: middle; text-align: center;">
      <img src="figs/jointsmoothingpost.svg" width="300">
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>


------------------------------------------------------------------------

## The Dirichlet Process {.theorem-slide}


@kowal2019dynamic



------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Chinese Restaurant Process

The DP induces a **clustering distribution** on observations. If $\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_{n-1} \sim G$ have been drawn, then:

$$\theta_n \mid \theta_1, \ldots, \theta_{n-1} \sim \frac{\alpha}{\alpha + n - 1} G_0 + \frac{1}{\alpha + n - 1} \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \delta_{\theta_i}$$

This is the **Pólya urn scheme**. Cluster structure: customer $n$ joins

| Event | Probability |
|------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Table $k$ (already occupied by $n_k$ customers) | $n_k / (\alpha + n - 1)$ |
| New table | $\alpha / (\alpha + n - 1)$ |

Expected number of clusters: $\mathbb{E}[K_n] \approx \alpha \log n$.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## DP Mixture Model

Combine the DP prior with a likelihood to get a **flexible density estimator**:

$$\begin{aligned}
G &\sim \mathrm{DP}(\alpha, G_0) \\
\phi_i \mid G &\overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} G \\
y_i \mid \phi_i &\sim f(y \mid \phi_i)
\end{aligned}$$

Marginalizing over $G$ gives a random partition model. With $G_0 = \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \kappa_0^{-1})$ and $f(y \mid \phi) = \mathcal{N}(y \mid \phi, \sigma^2)$ this is a **Gaussian DP mixture**.

::: highlight-box
The posterior $G \mid y_1, \ldots, y_n$ is again a DP:

$$G \mid y_{1:n} \sim \mathrm{DP}\!\left(\alpha + n,\; \frac{\alpha}{\alpha+n} G_0 + \frac{1}{\alpha+n}\sum_{i=1}^n \delta_{y_i}\right)$$
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Gibbs Sampler for DP Mixtures

Collapsed sampler (Neal 2000, Algorithm 3). Let $z_i \in \{1,\ldots,K\}$ be the cluster label for observation $i$.

For each $i = 1, \ldots, n$:

1.  Remove $i$ from its cluster; update counts $n_{-i,k}$
2.  Sample new label from:

$$p(z_i = k \mid z_{-i}, y) \propto \begin{cases}
n_{-i,k} \cdot p(y_i \mid y_{-i, k}) & k \text{ existing} \\[4pt]
\alpha \cdot p(y_i \mid G_0) & k \text{ new}
\end{cases}$$

where $p(y_i \mid y_{-i,k})$ is the posterior predictive under $G_0$.

For conjugate $G_0$ this is available in **closed form** — no MH step needed.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Implementation in Julia

```{julia}
#| label: lst-gibbs
#| eval: false

"""One full Gibbs sweep for a Gaussian DP mixture (conjugate NIG prior)."""
function gibbs_sweep!(z, y, α, μ₀, κ₀, a₀, b₀)
    n = length(y)
    for i in 1:n
        z[i] = 0                          # remove observation i
        k_vals, log_probs = Int[], Float64[]

        for k in unique(z[z .> 0])
            idx   = findall(==(k), z)
            nk    = length(idx)
            log_p = log(nk) + log_pred(y[i], y[idx], μ₀, κ₀, a₀, b₀)
            push!(k_vals, k); push!(log_probs, log_p)
        end

        # new cluster
        push!(k_vals, maximum(z) + 1)
        push!(log_probs, log(α) + log_pred(y[i], Float64[], μ₀, κ₀, a₀, b₀))

        probs  = softmax(log_probs)
        z[i]   = k_vals[rand(Categorical(probs))]
    end
    return z
end
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Dynamic shrinkage process priors - local level model

::: iframe-slide
<iframe width="100%" height="807" frameborder="0" src="https://observablehq.com/embed/@mattiasvillani/local-level-model-dsp-prior?cells=viewof+input%2Cviewof+plotselector%2Cviewof+simulatebutton%2Ctimeplot&amp;banner=false">

</iframe>
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Summary

::::: cols-2
<div>

**What we covered:**

-   DP is a prior over distributions with two parameters: $\alpha$ (concentration) and $G_0$ (base measure)
-   Stick-breaking gives a constructive representation
-   CRP describes the induced clustering
-   DP mixtures are flexible density estimators
-   Conjugate Gibbs sampler is clean and efficient

</div>

<div>

**Key formulas:**

| Object            | Expression                         |
|-------------------|------------------------------------|
| Prior mean        | $\mathbb{E}[G] = G_0$              |
| CRP new table     | $\alpha/(\alpha+n-1)$              |
| Posterior         | $\mathrm{DP}(\alpha+n, \tilde{G})$ |
| $\mathbb{E}[K_n]$ | $\approx \alpha \log n$            |

</div>
:::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## References {.smaller}
